mnemonics.title: Performance Metrics Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: Use RAPID: Response time, Availability, Performance, I/O metrics, Delay (latency)

mnemonics.title: Capacity Planning Steps
mnemonics.content: MAPS: Monitor current state, Analyze trends, Plan for future, Solution implementation

mnemonics.title: Bottleneck Identification
mnemonics.content: C P U: Check CPU first, then Performance issues reveal underlying problems, Understand disk and memory

acronyms.usage: Contract defining performance expectations like 99.9% uptime, response times
Maximum acceptable downtime before business impact becomes unacceptable
Maximum acceptable data loss measured in time (e.g., 1 hour of data)
Tools that monitor application code-level performance and transactions
Measurable values that demonstrate effectiveness in achieving objectives
Standard measure of system throughput for transaction processing systems

visualTips.visualization: Think of a highway: Response time is travel time for one car; Throughput is total cars reaching destination per hour. More cars (throughput) can cause congestion (increased response time)
Imagine a funnel - the narrow part (bottleneck) limits how fast you can pour liquid (throughput), regardless of how wide the top is
Think of a workplace - 0% is empty office, 100% is everyone working with no breaks. 80%+ means overtime needed (upgrade required)
Ping-pong ball drop - distance is the path, time to hit ground is latency. Lower height = lower latency
Building blocks - vertical is stacking taller (more resources), horizontal is adding more blocks (more servers)

analogies.analogy: Performance is like the speed of a car (how fast it goes), Capacity is like the size of the car (how many passengers). You can have a big car (high capacity) but drive slowly (poor performance)
Like a traffic signal at an intersection - distributes cars (requests) evenly to different roads (servers) to prevent congestion on any single route
Like keeping frequently used items on your desk instead of in a distant filing cabinet - faster access to commonly needed data
Like a checkout line at a supermarket - too few counters causes long waits (high latency), too many causes wasted resources (low utilization)
